The **South Downs** are a range of [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") hills that extends for about 260 sq mi (670 km2)[[1]](#cite_note-1) across the south-eastern coastal counties of England. This area of [downland](/wiki/Downland "Downland") extends from the [Itchen Valley](/wiki/River_Itchen,_Hampshire "River Itchen, Hampshire") of [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire") in the west to [Beachy Head](/wiki/Beachy_Head "Beachy Head"), in the [Eastbourne Downland Estate](/wiki/Eastbourne_Downland_Estate "Eastbourne Downland Estate") of East Sussex, in the east. The Downs are bounded on the northern side by a steep [escarpment](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment"), from whose crest there are extensive views northwards across the [Weald](/wiki/Weald "Weald") (which on its other side is bordered by the [North Downs](/wiki/North_Downs "North Downs")). The [South Downs National Park](/wiki/South_Downs_National_Park "South Downs National Park") forms a much larger area than the chalk range of the South Downs, and includes large parts of the Weald. The national park is the UK's most visited, attracting an estimated 39 million visitor-days annually.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The South Downs are characterised by rolling chalk downland with close-cropped turf and [dry valleys](/wiki/Dry_valley "Dry valley"), and are recognised as one of the most important chalk [landscapes](/wiki/Landscape "Landscape") in England.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The range is one of the four main areas of chalk [downland](/wiki/Downland "Downland") in southern [England](/wiki/England "England").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The South Downs are relatively less populated compared to [South East England](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England") as a whole, although there has been large-scale urban encroachment onto the chalk downland by major seaside resorts, including most notably [Brighton and Hove](/wiki/Brighton_and_Hove "Brighton and Hove"). The South Downs have been inhabited since ancient times and at periods the area has supported a large population, particularly during Romano-British times. There is a rich heritage of historical features and archaeological remains, including defensive sites, burial mounds and field boundaries. Within the South Downs Environmentally Sensitive Area there are thirty-seven [Sites of Special Scientific Interest](/wiki/Sites_of_Special_Scientific_Interest "Sites of Special Scientific Interest"), including large areas of chalk grassland.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The grazing of sheep on the thin, well-drained chalk soils of the Downs over many centuries, and browsing by rabbits, resulted in the fine, short, springy turf, known as *old chalk grassland*, that has come to epitomise the South Downs today. Until the middle of the 20th century, an agricultural system operated by downland farmers known as 'sheep-and-corn farming' underpinned this: the sheep (most famously the [Southdown](/wiki/Southdown_(sheep) "Southdown (sheep)") breed) of villagers would be systematically confined to certain corn fields to improve their fertility with their droppings and then they would be let out onto the downland to graze. However, starting in 1940 with government measures during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") to increase domestic food production – which continued into the 1950s - much grassland was ploughed up for arable farming, fundamentally changing the landscape and ecology, with the loss of much biodiversity. As a result, while old chalk grassland accounted for 40-50% of the eastern Downs before the war, only 3-4% survives.[[6]](#cite_note-6) This and development pressures from the surrounding population centres ultimately led to the decision to create the [South Downs National Park](/wiki/South_Downs_National_Park "South Downs National Park"), which came into full operation on 1 April 2011, to protect and restore the Downs.

The South Downs have also been designated as a [National Character Area](/wiki/National_Character_Area "National Character Area") (NCA 125) by [Natural England](/wiki/Natural_England "Natural England"). It is bordered by the [Hampshire Downs](/wiki/Hampshire_Downs "Hampshire Downs"), the [Wealden Greensand](/wiki/Wealden_Greensand "Wealden Greensand"), the [Low Weald](/wiki/Low_Weald "Low Weald") and the [Pevensey Levels](/wiki/Pevensey_Levels "Pevensey Levels") to the north and the [South Hampshire Lowlands](/wiki/South_Hampshire_Lowlands "South Hampshire Lowlands") and [South Coast Plain](/wiki/South_Coast_Plain "South Coast Plain") to the south.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The downland is a highly popular recreational destination, particularly for walkers, horseriders and mountain bikers. A [long distance footpath](/wiki/Long_distance_footpath "Long distance footpath") and [bridleway](/wiki/Bridleway "Bridleway"), the [South Downs Way](/wiki/South_Downs_Way "South Downs Way"), follows the entire length of the chalk ridge from Winchester to Eastbourne, complemented by many interconnecting public footpaths and bridleways.

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

This term is used to describe the characteristic landscape of 'elevated rolling grassy hills' in southern [England](/wiki/England "England"), where chalk and [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") is exposed at the surface, acquiring this sense around the 14th century.[[8]](#cite_note-Bettey_2002-8) The name "downs" is derived from the Celtic word "dun", meaning "[fort](/wiki/Hill_fort "Hill fort")" or "[fastness](/wiki/Fastness "Fastness")" (and by extension "fortified settlement", from which it entered English as "town", similar to Germanic ["burg"](/wiki/Burh "Burh")/["burough"](/wiki/Borough#Etymology "Borough")), though the original meaning would have been "hill", as early forts were commonly [hillforts](/wiki/Hillfort "Hillfort") - compare Germanic "burg" (fort) and "berg" (mountain).[[9]](#cite_note-9) These hills are prefixed 'south' to distinguish them from another chalk [escarpment](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment"), the [North Downs](/wiki/North_Downs "North Downs"), which runs roughly parallel to them about 30 mi (48 km) away on the northern edge of the Weald that divides the two ranges of hills.

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geology")]

Main article: [Geology of the South Downs National Park](/wiki/Geology_of_the_South_Downs_National_Park "Geology of the South Downs National Park")

[![Refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/S_East_Geology.jpg/250px-S_East_Geology.jpg)](/wiki/File:S_East_Geology.jpg)

Geological map of south-east England, with areas that have chalk bedrock shown in light-green.

[![Cross-sectional diagram of eroded layers of geological anticline with locations of towns indicated](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/KentGeologyWealdenDomeSimple.svg/250px-KentGeologyWealdenDomeSimple.svg.png)](/wiki/File:KentGeologyWealdenDomeSimple.svg)

A north-south cross-section through the Weald-Artois Anticline, East Sussex.

The South Downs are formed from a thick band of chalk which was deposited during the [Cretaceous Period](/wiki/Cretaceous_Period "Cretaceous Period") between 100 and 66 million years ago within a [shallow sea](/wiki/Shallow_sea "Shallow sea") which extended across much of northwest Europe. The [rock](/wiki/Rock_(geology) "Rock (geology)") is composed of the microscopic skeletons of [plankton](/wiki/Plankton "Plankton") which lived in the sea, hence its colour. The chalk has many [fossils](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil"), and bands of [flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") occur throughout the formation.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Chalk is divided into the Lower, Middle and Upper Chalk, a thin band of cream-coloured nodular chalk known as the Melbourn Rock marking the boundary between the Lower and Middle units.

The strata of southeast England, including the Chalk, were gently folded during a phase of the [Alpine Orogeny](/wiki/Alpine_Orogeny "Alpine Orogeny") to produce the [Weald-Artois Anticline](/wiki/Weald-Artois_Anticline "Weald-Artois Anticline"), a dome-like structure with a long east-west axis. [Erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion") has removed the central part of the dome, leaving the north-facing escarpment of the South Downs along its southern margin with the south-facing chalk escarpment of the [North Downs](/wiki/North_Downs "North Downs") as its counterpart on the northern side, as shown on the diagram. Between these two escarpments the anticline has been subject to differential erosion so that geologically distinct areas of hills and vales lie in roughly concentric circles towards the centre; these comprise the [Greensand Ridge](/wiki/Greensand_Ridge "Greensand Ridge"), most prominent on the north side of the Weald, where it includes [Leith Hill](/wiki/Leith_Hill "Leith Hill"), the highest hill in south-east England, the low-lying clay vales of the Low Weald, formed of less resistant [Weald Clay](/wiki/Weald_Clay "Weald Clay"), and finally the more highly resistant sandstones of the [High Weald](/wiki/High_Weald "High Weald") at the centre of the anticline, whose elevated forest ridge includes most notably [Ashdown Forest](/wiki/Ashdown_Forest "Ashdown Forest").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The chalk, being porous, allows water to soak through; as a result, there are many [winterbournes](/wiki/Winterbourne_(stream) "Winterbourne (stream)") along the northern edge.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

The South Downs are a long chalk escarpment that stretches for over 110 km (68 mi), rising from the valley of the [River Itchen](/wiki/River_Itchen,_Hampshire "River Itchen, Hampshire") near Winchester, [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire"), in the west to [Beachy Head](/wiki/Beachy_Head "Beachy Head") near [Eastbourne](/wiki/Eastbourne "Eastbourne"), [East Sussex](/wiki/East_Sussex "East Sussex"), in the east.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Behind the steep north-facing scarp slope, the gently inclined [dip slope](/wiki/Dip_slope "Dip slope") of undulating chalk downland extends for a distance of up to 7 mi (11 km) southwards. Viewed from high points further north in the [High Weald](/wiki/High_Weald "High Weald") and on the North Downs, the scarp of the South Downs presents itself as a steep wall that bounds the horizon, with its grassland heights punctuated with clumps of trees (such as [Chanctonbury Ring](/wiki/Chanctonbury_Ring "Chanctonbury Ring")).

In the west, the chalk ridge of the South Downs merges with the [North Downs](/wiki/North_Downs "North Downs") to form the [Hampshire Downs](/wiki/Hampshire_Downs "Hampshire Downs"). In the east, the escarpment terminates at the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") coast between [Seaford](/wiki/Seaford,_East_Sussex "Seaford, East Sussex") and [Eastbourne](/wiki/Eastbourne "Eastbourne"), where it produces the spectacular white cliffs of [Seaford Head](/wiki/Seaford_Head "Seaford Head"), the cross-section of dry valleys known as the [Seven Sisters](/wiki/Seven_Sisters,_Sussex "Seven Sisters, Sussex") and [Beachy Head](/wiki/Beachy_Head "Beachy Head"), the highest chalk sea cliff in Britain at 162 m (531 ft) above sea level.

The South Downs may be said to have three main component parts: the East Hampshire Downs, the Western Downs and the Eastern Downs, together with the river valleys that cut across them and the land immediately below them, the *scarpfoot*.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrandon19981–3-13) The Western and Eastern Downs are often collectively referred to as the *Sussex Downs*. The Western Downs, lying west of the [River Arun](/wiki/River_Arun "River Arun"), are much more wooded, particularly on the scarp face, than the Eastern Downs. The bare Eastern Downs – the only part of the chalk escarpment to which, until the late 19th century, the term "South Downs" was usually applied – have come to epitomise, in literature and art, the South Downs as a whole and which have been the subject matter of such celebrated writers and artists as [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling") (the "blunt, bow-headed, whale-backed downs") and [Eric Ravilious](/wiki/Eric_Ravilious "Eric Ravilious").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrandon19983-14)

Four river valleys cut through the South Downs, namely those of the rivers Arun, [Adur](/wiki/River_Adur "River Adur"), [Ouse](/wiki/River_Ouse,_Sussex "River Ouse, Sussex") and [Cuckmere](/wiki/Cuckmere "Cuckmere"), providing a contrasting landscape. Chalk [aquifers](/wiki/Aquifer "Aquifer") and to a lesser extent [winterbourne streams](/wiki/Winterbourne_(stream) "Winterbourne (stream)") supply much of the water required by the surrounding settlements. [Dew ponds](/wiki/Dew_pond "Dew pond"), artificial ponds for watering livestock, are a characteristic feature on the downland.

The highest point on the South Downs is [Butser Hill](/wiki/Butser_Hill "Butser Hill"), whose summit is 271 m (889 ft) above sea level. The plateau-like top of this irregularly shaped hill, which lies just south of [Petersfield, Hampshire](/wiki/Petersfield,_Hampshire "Petersfield, Hampshire"), was in regular use through prehistory. It has been designated as a [national nature reserve](/wiki/National_nature_reserves_in_England "National nature reserves in England").

Within the boundary of the South Downs National Park, which includes parts of the western Weald to the north of the South Downs, the highest point is [Blackdown](/wiki/Blackdown,_West_Sussex "Blackdown, West Sussex"), West Sussex, which rises to 280 m (919 ft) above sea level. However, Blackdown geologically is not part of the South Downs but instead forms part of the [Greensand Ridge](/wiki/Greensand_Ridge "Greensand Ridge") on the [Weald](/wiki/Weald "Weald")'s western margins.

A list of those points on the South Downs above 700 ft (210 m), going from west to east, is given below.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Cissbury1.jpg/330px-Cissbury1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cissbury1.jpg)

Plan of Cissbury fort showing shafts

Archaeological evidence has revealed that the Downs have been inhabited and utilised for thousands of years. [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") flint mines such as [Cissbury](/wiki/Cissbury "Cissbury"), [burial mounds](/wiki/Burial_mounds "Burial mounds") such as the [Devil's Jumps](/wiki/Devil%27s_Jumps,_Treyford "Devil's Jumps, Treyford") and [Devil's Humps](/wiki/Devil%27s_Humps "Devil's Humps"), and [hill forts](/wiki/Hill_forts "Hill forts") like [Chanctonbury Ring](/wiki/Chanctonbury_Ring "Chanctonbury Ring") are strong features in the landscape.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

It has been estimated that the tree cover of the Downs was cleared over 3000 years ago, and the present closely grazed turf is the result of continual [grazing](/wiki/Grazing "Grazing") by sheep.

## National park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: National park")]

Main article: [South Downs National Park](/wiki/South_Downs_National_Park "South Downs National Park")

Proposals to create a national park for the South Downs date back to the 1940s. However, it was not until 1999 that the idea received firm government support. After a public enquiry that took place between 2003 and 2009, the government announced its decision to make the South Downs a national park on 31 March 2009. The [South Downs National Park](/wiki/South_Downs_National_Park "South Downs National Park") finally came into operation on 1 April 2011. Within its boundary are included not only the South Downs proper but also part of the [western Weald](/wiki/Western_Weald "Western Weald"), a geologically and ecologically quite different district.

The South Downs National Park has replaced two [Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty (AONB)s](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty"): [East Hampshire AONB](/wiki/East_Hampshire_AONB "East Hampshire AONB") and [Sussex Downs AONB](/wiki/Sussex_Downs_AONB "Sussex Downs AONB"). During the enquiry process a number of boundary questions were considered, so that the National Park contains areas not in the former AONBs, and vice versa.

## National nature reserves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: National nature reserves")]

[National nature reserves](/wiki/National_nature_reserves_in_England "National nature reserves in England") (NNRs)[[17]](#cite_note-17) on the Sussex Downs comprise [Kingley Vale](/wiki/Kingley_Vale "Kingley Vale"), near [Chichester](/wiki/Chichester "Chichester"), said by [Natural England](/wiki/Natural_England "Natural England") to contain one of the finest yew forests in Europe, including a grove of ancient trees which are among the oldest living things in Britain (the reserve is also one of the most important archaeological sites in southern England, with 14 [scheduled monuments](/wiki/Scheduled_monument "Scheduled monument")); [Castle Hill](/wiki/Castle_Hill,_Brighton "Castle Hill, Brighton"), between Brighton and [Lewes](/wiki/Lewes "Lewes"), an important example of ancient, traditionally managed grassland; Lewes Downs ([Mount Caburn](/wiki/Mount_Caburn "Mount Caburn")), a traditionally managed chalk downland (and also an important archaeological site); and [Lullington Heath](/wiki/Lullington_Heath "Lullington Heath"), on the northern fringe of Friston Forest north-west of Eastbourne, one of the largest areas of chalk heath in Britain.

The NNRs on the East Hampshire Downs comprise [Butser Hill](/wiki/Butser_Hill "Butser Hill"), near Petersfield, a large area of chalk grassland on the highest point in the South Downs (a large area is also designated as a scheduled monument reflecting its historical significance, particularly in the Bronze and Iron Ages); [Old Winchester Hill](/wiki/Old_Winchester_Hill "Old Winchester Hill"), a lowland grassland on the west and south facing scarp slopes of the Meon valley; and Beacon Hill, a high quality chalk grassland 5 km west of Old Winchester Hill.

## Tourism, leisure and sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Tourism, leisure and sport")]

In 1923 the Society of Sussex Downsmen (now the South Downs Society) was formed with the aim of protecting the area's unique landscape.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The South Downs are a popular area for ramblers with a network of over 2,000 mi (3,200 km) of well-managed, well-signed and easily accessible trails. The principal bridleway, and longest of them, is the [South Downs Way](/wiki/South_Downs_Way "South Downs Way").[[19]](#cite_note-19) The [Monarch's Way](/wiki/Monarch%27s_Way "Monarch's Way"), having originated at [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England"), crosses the South Downs and ends at [Shoreham-by-Sea](/wiki/Shoreham-by-Sea "Shoreham-by-Sea").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Sports undertaken on the Downs include [paragliding](/wiki/Paragliding "Paragliding"), [mountain-biking](/wiki/Mountain-biking "Mountain-biking"), [horse riding](/wiki/Horse_riding "Horse riding") and [walking](/wiki/Walking "Walking").[[21]](#cite_note-21) The popular *Beachy Head Marathon* (formerly *Seven Sisters Marathon*), a hilly cross-country marathon, takes place each autumn on the eastern Downs, starting and finishing in Eastbourne. The South Downs Trail Marathon starts in the village of Slindon (near Arundel) and ends at the Queen Elizabeth Country Park (to the south of Petersfield.)

Longer events that take place in the South Downs Way include a 100-mile running 'ultramarathon' and a 75-mile mountain biking night time race from Beachy Head to Queen Elizabeth Country Park.

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Long_Man_of_Wilmington.jpg/250px-Long_Man_of_Wilmington.jpg)](/wiki/File:Long_Man_of_Wilmington.jpg)

The [Long Man of Wilmington](/wiki/Long_Man_of_Wilmington "Long Man of Wilmington"), inscribed into the scarp face of the South Downs in [East Sussex](/wiki/East_Sussex "East Sussex")

Three of the landmarks on the Downs are the [Long Man of Wilmington](/wiki/Long_Man_of_Wilmington "Long Man of Wilmington") and the [Litlington White Horse](/wiki/Litlington_White_Horse "Litlington White Horse") being chalk carved [hill figures](/wiki/Hill_figure "Hill figure"), and [Clayton Windmills](/wiki/Clayton_Windmills "Clayton Windmills"). There is also a [war memorial](/wiki/War_memorial "War memorial"), [The Chattri](/wiki/Chattri,_Brighton "Chattri, Brighton"), dedicated to [Indian soldiers](/wiki/Indian_soldiers "Indian soldiers") who died in the [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton") area, having been brought there for treatment after being injured fighting on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") in the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War").

## South Downs in literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=South_Downs&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: South Downs in literature")]

[Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling"), who lived at [Bateman's](/wiki/Bateman%27s "Bateman's"), described the South Downs as "Our blunt, bow-headed whale-backed Downs".[[22]](#cite_note-22) Writing in 1920 in his poem *The South Country*, poet [Hilaire Belloc](/wiki/Hilaire_Belloc "Hilaire Belloc") describes the South Downs as "the great hills of the South Country".[[23]](#cite_note-23) In *On The South Coast*, poet [Algernon Charles Swinburne](/wiki/Algernon_Charles_Swinburne "Algernon Charles Swinburne") describes the South Downs as "the green smooth-swelling unending downs".[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The naturalist-writer [William Henry Hudson](/wiki/William_Henry_Hudson "William Henry Hudson") wrote that "during the whole fifty-three mile length from Beachy Head to Harting the ground never rises above a height of 850 feet, but we feel on top of the world".[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Poet [Francis William Bourdillon](/wiki/Francis_William_Bourdillon "Francis William Bourdillon") also wrote a poem "On the South Downs".[[26]](#cite_note-26) The South Downs have been home to several writers, including [Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen") who lived at [Chawton](/wiki/Chawton "Chawton") on the edge of the Downs in Hampshire. The [Bloomsbury Group](/wiki/Bloomsbury_Group "Bloomsbury Group") often visited [Monk's House](/wiki/Monk%27s_House "Monk's House") in [Rodmell](/wiki/Rodmell "Rodmell"), the home of [Virginia Woolf](/wiki/Virginia_Woolf "Virginia Woolf") in the Ouse valley. Alfred, [Lord Tennyson](/wiki/Lord_Tennyson "Lord Tennyson"), had a second home at Aldworth, on [Blackdown](/wiki/Blackdown,_Sussex "Blackdown, Sussex"); geologically part of the [Weald](/wiki/Weald "Weald"), Blackdown lies north of the South Downs but is included in the South Downs National Park.

In the introduction to [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle")'s short story collection *His Last Bow*, Dr. Watson states that [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes") has retired to a small farm upon the Downs near Eastbourne. In the story "His Last Bow" itself, Holmes states that he "lives and keeps bees upon the South Downs". Furthermore, the short story "[The Lion's Mane](/wiki/The_Lion%27s_Mane "The Lion's Mane")" is about a case that Holmes solves whilst living there.

The author [Graham Greene](/wiki/Graham_Greene "Graham Greene")'s first published novel, *[The Man Within](/wiki/The_Man_Within "The Man Within")* (1929), is set largely on and around the South Downs. The book's principal character, Andrews, travels by foot across the Downs to reach [Lewes](/wiki/Lewes "Lewes") and attend the Assizes. Greene provides a detailed description of both the landscape and its 'feel'.

The author [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") published a book called *The Invisible Man* in 1898, and in it says "He fled to South Down".
